title: Cannot read properties of undefined
why: You're accessing a property on a variable that is undefined. This usually happens when an API response hasn't loaded yet or an object key is misspelled.
fixes: Add optional chaining: obj?.property
Add null check: if (obj != null) { obj.property }
Provide default value: const val = obj?.prop ?? fallback

title: TypeError: X is not a function
why: You're calling something as a function that isn't one. Common causes: wrong import, calling a property that's a value not a method, or the object hasn't been initialized yet.
fixes: Check the variable type: typeof fn === 'function' && fn()
Verify the import path is correct
Ensure the method exists on the object

title: Cannot set properties of undefined
why: You're trying to assign a value to a property on something that is undefined or null. The parent object doesn't exist yet.
fixes: Initialize the parent object first: obj = obj || {}
Use nullish coalescing assignment: obj ??= {}
Check the chain of property access for undefined links

title: TypeError: Assignment to constant variable
why: You declared a variable with 'const' which makes it read-only, then tried to reassign it. Const bindings cannot be changed after initialization.
fixes: Change const to let if the variable needs reassignment
Use a new variable for the new value
For objects/arrays, mutate properties instead of reassigning: obj.key = val

title: SyntaxError: Unexpected token in JSON
why: JSON.parse() received a string that isn't valid JSON. Common causes: the server returned HTML (like a 404 page), there's a trailing comma, or single quotes were used instead of double quotes.
fixes: Check the raw response: console.log(typeof response, response)
Validate the JSON string at jsonlint.com
Ensure the server returns Content-Type: application/json

title: RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded
why: A function is calling itself recursively without a proper base case, creating an infinite loop that exhausts the call stack.
fixes: Add or fix the base case in your recursive function
Check for circular references in objects being stringified
Convert recursion to iteration with a stack/queue

title: ReferenceError: Cannot access before initialization
why: You're using a let/const variable before its declaration in the code. Unlike var, let/const have a 'temporal dead zone' from the start of the block to the declaration.
fixes: Move the variable declaration before its first usage
Check for variables with the same name in different scopes
Ensure imports are at the top of the file

title: ReferenceError: X is not defined
why: You're using a variable or function name that doesn't exist in the current scope. It was never declared, is misspelled, or is out of scope.
fixes: Check for typos in the variable name
Ensure the variable is imported or declared in scope
Verify script load order in the HTML

title: TypeError: X.forEach is not a function
why: You're calling .forEach() on something that isn't an array. It could be an object, NodeList (in older browsers), null, or undefined.
fixes: Convert to array first: Array.from(value).forEach(...)
Check the value type: Array.isArray(value) && value.forEach(...)
Use Object.entries(obj).forEach() for objects

title: SyntaxError: Unexpected end of input
why: The parser reached the end of the string/file while still expecting more content. Usually a missing closing bracket, parenthesis, or the response body was empty.
fixes: Check for missing closing brackets/braces/parentheses
Verify the fetch response isn't empty before parsing
Use a code formatter to find unmatched delimiters

pattern: Cannot convert undefined or null to object
title: TypeError: Cannot convert undefined or null to object
why: You passed undefined or null to a function that expects an object, like Object.keys(), Object.assign(), or the spread operator on a non-object.
fixes: Add a fallback: Object.keys(obj || {})
Guard with a check: if (obj) { Object.keys(obj) }
Ensure the data source returns an object, not undefined

title: TypeError: Failed to fetch
why: The browser couldn't complete the network request. Common causes: CORS blocking, server is down, no internet, or the URL is wrong.
fixes: Check browser DevTools Network tab for the actual status
Verify CORS headers on the server: Access-Control-Allow-Origin
Ensure the URL is correct and the server is running

pattern: Reduce of empty array with no initial value
title: TypeError: Reduce of empty array with no initial value
why: You called .reduce() on an empty array without providing an initial value as the second argument. When the array is empty, reduce doesn't know what to return.
fixes: Provide an initial value: arr.reduce((acc, val) => acc + val, 0)
Guard against empty arrays: if (arr.length) { arr.reduce(...) }
Use optional chaining with a fallback

title: SyntaxError: Illegal return statement
why: You used a 'return' statement outside of a function body. This often happens when braces are mismatched or code is pasted at the top level.
fixes: Ensure return is inside a function or method
Check for mismatched curly braces above the return
Wrap top-level code in an IIFE or function if needed

title: TypeError: X is not iterable
why: You used for...of, destructuring, or spread on a value that doesn't implement the iterable protocol (like a plain object, number, or undefined).
fixes: Check the value isn't undefined/null before iterating
Use Object.entries() for objects: for (const [k,v] of Object.entries(obj))
Ensure the function returns an array, not a single value

title: IndentationError: unexpected indent
why: Python uses indentation to define code blocks. You have extra spaces or tabs where they shouldn't be, or you've mixed tabs and spaces inconsistently.
fixes: Use consistent indentation (4 spaces per level, no tabs)
Configure your editor to show whitespace characters
Run: python -tt script.py to detect tab/space mixing

title: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named X
why: Python can't find the module you're importing. It's either not installed, installed in a different environment, or the module name is misspelled.
fixes: Install the package: pip install <package-name>
Check you're using the right virtual environment
Verify module name matches PyPI name (e.g., pip install Pillow, import PIL)

title: TypeError: argument of type 'NoneType'
why: You're performing an operation on a None value, usually because a function returned None implicitly (no return statement) or an assignment like list.sort() returns None.
fixes: Check the function's return value: result = fn(); if result is not None: ...
Note that .sort(), .append(), .update() return None — they modify in-place
Add explicit return statements in your functions

title: KeyError: X
why: You're accessing a dictionary key that doesn't exist. The key is misspelled, wasn't added yet, or was already removed.
fixes: Use .get() with a default: data.get('key', default_value)
Check key existence: if 'key' in data: ...
Use collections.defaultdict for auto-initializing dicts

title: AttributeError: object has no attribute X
why: You're accessing a method or property that doesn't exist on this object. The object might be the wrong type, or the attribute name is misspelled.
fixes: Check the object type: print(type(obj))
Use hasattr(obj, 'attr') before accessing
Verify the correct class/module is imported

title: ValueError: too many values to unpack
why: You're destructuring an iterable into fewer variables than it contains. The left side has fewer names than the right side has values.
fixes: Use * to capture extras: a, b, *rest = iterable
Match the number of variables to the iterable length
Use indexing instead: a = iterable[0]

title: TypeError: missing required positional argument
why: You called a function without providing all required arguments. If it's a method, you may have forgotten 'self', or called Class.method() instead of instance.method().
fixes: Pass all required arguments to the function
For methods, call on an instance: obj.method() not Class.method()
Check the function signature for required parameters

title: RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
why: A function keeps calling itself without stopping. The base case is missing or never reached, causing infinite recursion until Python's stack limit (usually 1000) is hit.
fixes: Add or fix the base case in your recursive function
Convert to iterative approach with a loop
If intentional deep recursion: sys.setrecursionlimit(n) (use carefully)

title: TypeError: 'int' object is not subscriptable/iterable
why: You're using [] indexing or iterating over an integer. A variable you expected to be a list/string is actually a number, often from an accidental reassignment.
fixes: Check the variable type: print(type(var))
Trace where the variable gets reassigned to an int
Ensure function returns a list/string, not a count or status code

title: ImportError: cannot import name X from Y
why: The name you're importing doesn't exist in the module. It could be a circular import, a renamed/removed function, or a version mismatch.
fixes: Check circular imports: A imports from B which imports from A
Verify the name exists in the module: dir(module)
Check you have the correct package version installed

title: TS2322: Type X is not assignable to type Y
why: TypeScript detected a type mismatch. You're assigning a value of one type to a variable/parameter that expects a different type. The compiler is preventing a potential runtime error.
fixes: Fix the value to match the expected type
Update the type annotation to accept the value
Use a type assertion if you're certain: value as ExpectedType

title: TS2339: Property X does not exist on type Y
why: You're accessing a property that TypeScript doesn't know about on this type. The type definition is missing it, or the variable has a wider/narrower type than expected.
fixes: Add the property to the interface/type definition
Use type narrowing: if ('prop' in obj) { obj.prop }
Cast to the correct type: (obj as ExtendedType).prop

title: TS2532: Object is possibly undefined/null
why: TypeScript's strict null checks found a code path where a value could be undefined or null. You need to handle the null case before using the value.
fixes: Add optional chaining: obj?.property
Add a null guard: if (obj) { obj.property }
Use non-null assertion if certain: obj!.property (not recommended)

pattern: Argument of type.*is not assignable to parameter of type
title: TS2345: Argument type mismatch
why: A function call is receiving an argument of the wrong type. The value you're passing doesn't match what the function's parameter expects.
fixes: Transform the argument to the expected type before passing
Update the function signature to accept the broader type
Check if you're passing the right variable

pattern: Cannot find module.*or its corresponding type declarations
title: TS2307: Cannot find module or type declarations
why: TypeScript can't resolve the import. Either the package isn't installed, it lacks type definitions, or the path alias isn't configured in tsconfig.
fixes: Install types: npm install -D @types/package-name
Create a declaration file: declare module 'package-name'
Check tsconfig paths and baseUrl configuration

title: TS2305: Module has no exported member
why: You're importing a name that isn't exported from the module. It may have been renamed, moved to a different file, or is a default export being imported as named.
fixes: Check the module's exports: use IDE auto-import
Use default import if it's a default export
Verify the package version matches the import syntax

title: TS2739: Type is missing required properties
why: You're creating an object that doesn't have all required properties defined in its type. Some fields are mandatory but weren't included.
fixes: Add the missing properties to the object literal
Make properties optional in the type: prop?: string
Use Partial<Type> if all properties should be optional

pattern: Conversion of type.*may be a mistake
title: TS2352: Type assertion may be a mistake
why: TypeScript thinks your type assertion (as X) doesn't make sense because the types have no overlap. The source and target types are incompatible.
fixes: Double-assert through unknown: value as unknown as TargetType
Fix the source type to be compatible with the target
Use a type guard function instead of assertion

title: TS2314: Generic type requires type arguments
why: You're using a generic type without specifying its type parameters. TypeScript needs to know the concrete types to use.
fixes: Provide type arguments: Array<string>, Map<string, number>
Let TypeScript infer when possible by passing a value
Add default type parameters to the generic: T = unknown

title: TS6133: Variable is declared but never used
why: You declared a variable, import, or parameter that's never referenced in the code. This usually indicates dead code or a forgotten refactor.
fixes: Remove the unused declaration
Prefix with underscore if intentionally unused: _unusedParam
Disable with // @ts-ignore or configure noUnusedLocals in tsconfig

title: Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory
why: Node.js tried to access a file or directory that doesn't exist at the specified path. The path may be wrong, relative to an unexpected directory, or the file was deleted.
fixes: Use path.resolve() or path.join() for reliable paths
Check existence first: fs.existsSync(path)
Create parent dirs: fs.mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true })

title: Error: EADDRINUSE: address already in use
why: Another process is already listening on the port you're trying to use. A previous instance of your server may not have shut down cleanly.
fixes: Kill the process on that port: npx kill-port 3000
Use a different port: PORT=3001 node server.js
Find the process: netstat -ano | findstr :3000 (Windows) or lsof -i :3000 (Mac/Linux)

title: Error: require() of ES Module not supported
why: You're using require() to load a package that only supports ES modules (import/export). The package dropped CommonJS support in a recent version.
fixes: Use dynamic import: const pkg = await import('package')
Add "type": "module" to package.json and use import syntax
Downgrade to the last CJS-compatible version of the package

title: FATAL ERROR: JavaScript heap out of memory
why: Node.js ran out of memory. You're processing too much data in memory at once, have a memory leak, or the default heap limit (~1.7GB) is too low for your workload.
fixes: Increase heap: node --max-old-space-size=4096 script.js
Use streams for large files instead of reading all into memory
Check for memory leaks: growing arrays, unclosed connections, event listener accumulation

title: Error: Cannot find module
why: Node.js can't locate the module at the specified path. The package isn't installed, the file path is wrong, or node_modules is corrupted.
fixes: Run npm install to restore dependencies
Delete node_modules and package-lock.json, then npm install
Check the import path for typos and correct casing

pattern: Rendered more hooks than during the previous render
title: React: Rendered more hooks than previous render
why: You're calling hooks conditionally or inside loops. React relies on hooks being called in the same order every render. An early return or conditional before a hook breaks this.
fixes: Move all hooks above any early returns or conditions
Never put hooks inside if/else, loops, or nested functions
Extract conditional logic into a child component

title: React: Too many re-renders
why: A component is stuck in an infinite render loop. Usually caused by calling setState directly in the render body or passing a function call instead of a function reference to an event handler.
fixes: Use arrow function in handler: onClick={() => setCount(count+1)} not onClick={setCount(count+1)}
Move setState into useEffect with proper dependencies
Check that useEffect dependencies don't cause cascading updates

pattern: Each child in a list should have a unique.*key.*prop
title: React: Each child in a list should have a unique key
why: React needs a stable, unique 'key' prop on each element in a list to efficiently track which items changed, were added, or removed during re-renders.
fixes: Add a unique key from your data: key={item.id}
Never use array index as key if list order can change
Use a combination of fields if no single unique ID: key={`${item.name}-${item.date}`}

pattern: Cannot update a component.*while rendering a different component
title: React: Cannot update component while rendering another
why: You're calling setState of a parent component during the render phase of a child component. React doesn't allow state updates that trigger re-renders during an ongoing render.
fixes: Move the state update to useEffect
Use a callback pattern or event-based communication
Lift state up or use context to avoid cross-component updates during render

pattern: Invalid hook call.*Hooks can only be called inside
title: React: Invalid hook call
why: You're calling a hook (useState, useEffect, etc.) outside a React function component or custom hook. This can also happen with duplicate React copies in node_modules.
fixes: Ensure hooks are only called in function components or custom hooks
Check for multiple React versions: npm ls react
Don't call hooks in class components, event handlers, or regular functions

title: fatal: not a git repository
why: You ran a git command in a directory that isn't inside a git repository. Either you're in the wrong folder, or git init was never run here.
fixes: Navigate to the correct project directory: cd /path/to/repo
Initialize a new repo: git init
Check if .git folder exists in parent directories

title: CONFLICT: Merge conflict in file
why: Two branches modified the same lines in a file differently. Git can't automatically decide which version to keep, so it marks the conflict for you to resolve manually.
fixes: Open the file and resolve conflict markers (<<<<<<, ======, >>>>>>)
Use a merge tool: git mergetool
After resolving: git add <file> && git commit

title: error: failed to push some refs
why: The remote branch has commits that your local branch doesn't have. Someone else pushed changes, or you rebased/amended commits that were already pushed.
fixes: Pull first: git pull --rebase origin branch-name
If intentional: git push --force-with-lease (safer than --force)
Fetch and merge: git fetch origin && git merge origin/branch

pattern: Your local changes.*would be overwritten by
title: error: Your local changes would be overwritten
why: You have uncommitted changes in files that the checkout/pull/merge needs to modify. Git refuses to overwrite your work silently.
fixes: Stash changes: git stash && git pull && git stash pop
Commit your changes first: git add . && git commit -m 'wip'
Discard changes if not needed: git checkout -- <file>

title: You are in 'detached HEAD' state
why: You checked out a specific commit, tag, or remote branch directly instead of a local branch. Any new commits won't belong to any branch and could be lost.
fixes: Create a branch from current state: git checkout -b new-branch-name
Return to a branch: git checkout main
If you already made commits: git branch save-my-work && git checkout main
